3: During the last decades, world faces the increasing problems of food safety and climate change (McMichael et al., 2006; Gerber et al., 2013). In this context, the environmental impact of live- stock production has received growing attention due to its competition for scarce resources, such as land, water and energy, and associated emissions to air, water and soil (Steinfeld et al., 2006; de Vries and de Boer, 2010). The life cycle stages of live- stock products (such as production, conservation and distribution) require large amounts of energy, which are translated into negative environmental impacts (Roy et al., 2009). Indeed, it is known that 14.5% of anthropogenic emissions of greenhouse gases (GHGs) are due to the animal husbandry sector (Gerber et al., 2013). In this sense, consumers show increasing interest to the origin, quality

and/or environmental impact of food production, with a change towards more sustainable systems within the sector (Notarnicola et al., 2012). In this context, meat consumption is consistently growing worldwide (by 63% during the last 40 years) as a result of the highest demand by emerging countries (FAO, 2004; Ciolos, 2012). The main challenge of the livestock sector is, therefore, to meet the increasing demand but simultaneously reduce the related environmental impacts. In Europe, the main types of meat: beef and veal, pigmeat, chicken, sheep and goat, account for one quarter of the total value of agricultural production (European Commission, 2004). Pigmeat is the main type of meat produced in Europe as well as the most widely consumed in the world (in kg product), achieving more than 106 million tons in 2009 (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012), with a foreseen increase of its production by 2050 up to 40% (FAO, 2011). However, pigmeat production is also the second contributor of GHGs emission from livestock sector after beef, with about 9% of total emissions (Gerber et al., 2013). The environmental impact of this sector is the result of GHG emissions from manure
management and enteric fermentation, fossil fuel combustion, deforestation and even from the application of fertilisers for crop cultivation (Steinfeld et al., 2006).

Accordingly, it could be concluded that intensive pigmeat pro- duction represents a crucial issue in ensuring sustainability in meat production. Therefore, it is necessary to assess the environmental impacts and identify input parameters that have a large influence on the pigmeat supply chain (Reckmann et al., 2013; Philippe and Nicks, 2014; Groen et al., 2016). Different environmental assess- ment methods are available to carry out this task. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), an internationally accepted and standardised holistic method, has been widely applied and several LCA studies are available on pigmeat production systems in Europe (Basset- Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Basset-Mens et al., 2007; Dalgaard et al., 2007; Nguyen et al., 2010; Lehmann et al., 2011; Reckmann et al., 2012, 2013; Dourmad et al., 2014; Philippe and Nicks, 2014; Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Van Zanten et al., 2015; Groen et al., 2016; McAuliffe, 2016). The available reports identified feed pro- duction as the main contributor to the impact categories assessed, followed by pig housing stage, mainly due to the manure man- agement schemes. Moreover, fossil energy use and GHG emissions associated to pigmeat production could be reduced by taking improvement measures in feed use and manure management practices (Nguyen et al., 2010; Groen et al., 2016).

The goal of this study was to evaluate the environmental bur- dens of pigmeat production by means of LCA methodology. To do so, primary data provided by two separated farms representative of the pigmeat sector in Galicia (Northwest Spain) were collected and assessed in detail. The estimation of the environmental impacts and the identification of environmental hotspots were performed. Accordingly, and taking into account its significant contribution to the environmental results, a detailed analysis was carried out in terms of concentrate feed production. Alternative scenarios to the current concentrate feed formulations were also proposed in order to evaluate their potential environmental credits. Moreover, life cycle inventory data was also used to calculate the edible protein energy return on investment: ep-EROI. It is a dimensionless ratio that allows estimating the energy efficiency of a productive system, since it studies the proportionality between the energy re- quirements for food production and the energy that the product provides human consumers.

8: The principles and guidelines established by ISO standards (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006) were considered in this study following a life cycle approach.
9: The goal of this study was to identify and evaluate the envi- ronmental burdens associated with pigmeat production in North- west Spain through a LCA approach. Moreover, once feed production was identified as the main hotspot in the production chain, different feeding strategies were proposed as alternatives to mitigate the environmental impacts related to the entire system.

The study was performed through a cradle-to-farm gate perspective; therefore, further stages of slaughtering, packaging, distribution and meat consumption were excluded from the assessment. Most previous LCA studies involving pigmeat produc- tion also comprised the slaughtering stage within the system boundaries (Dalgaard et al., 2007; Nguyen et al., 2011; Reckmann et al., 2013; González-García et al., 2015). However, the hotspots in all these studies were recorded in the stages before the slaughterhouse.
10: The function of this study was focused on rearing pigs. Accordingly, the functional unit (FU) was defined as "100 kg live weight of pig at farm gate", which is the average live weight of pigs before slaughterhouse in this study (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Dalgaard et al., 2007; Nguyen et al., 2010; Reckmann et al., 2013; Dourmad et al., 2014).
11: <header>2.3. System boundaries and description of pigmeat production system </header>

The total of processes or activities involved in the system under study is displayed in Fig. 1. Thus, all activities related to crop cultivation and concentrate feed production, distribution to the farms, gestation and lactation stages, rearing of piglets until weaning and post-weaning piglets as well as fattening pigs were encompassed in this study. Moreover, emissions to soil, air and water derived from the entire system were included within the system boundaries.

The production system in this study was divided into two main subsystems (Fig. off-farm concentrate feed production (S1) and pig farming (S2). In addition, off-farm concentrate feed production subsystem was constituted by three main stages: SOWS concentrate feed production (S1.1), weaning concentrate feed production (S1.2) and fattening concentrate feed production (S1.3) as well as pig farming subsystem, divided into weaning farm (S2.1) and fattening farm (S2.2).
12: A total of six different concentrate feed mixtures (mainly focused on protein content) were used at farms in accordance with the physiological stage of pigs. These mixtures were the following: gestating concentrate, lactating concentrate, first starter concen- trate, second starter concentrate, first finisher concentrate and second finisher concentrate. Detailed information on ingredients and compositions is presented in Table 1.

Concentrate feed production subsystem comprises all the ac- tivities carried out directly during crops cultivation, production of additives and other concentrate feed ingredients, transport of in- gredients from cropland and final animal feed production (Fig. 1). Thus, inputs production and use (seeds, agrochemicals and fossil fuels) and their derived emissions were included within the sub- system boundaries. Emissions and resources associated with en- ergy use and water consumption for the processes related to the final concentrate feed production stage were also considered.
13: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

After initial insemination, SOWS remain housed in the gestating section during approximately 110 days and then, they are moved to the farrowing section, 5 days before the provisional farrowing date. Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Due to the young age of piglets, optimal ambient conditions must be provided; thus, both electrical and thermal energy

Fig. 1. Scheme of the system boundaries for the pigs rearing system under assessment (Base Case - Scenario A). Black box represents the process excluded from the assessment. "Avoided mineral fertilisation" process includes both avoided environmental burdens due to mineral fertiliser production and emissions derived from its application to soil.
consumptions arise as key factors in the weaning stage (weaning farm).

Regarding feeding, piglets are fed for about 35 days with ratios of 105 g of first starter concentrate-day-1 piglet-¹ and 415 g of second starter concentrate-day piglet-1. The compositions of concentrate feed used in the weaning stage are displayed in Table 1.

2.3.2.2. Fattening farm. After the first stage, weaned piglets are transported (115 km far) to the fattening farm located in Lalin (08° 07' long. W, 42° 39 lat. N) with an extension around 2 ha. This second farm also operates under cycles, completing one fattening stage during each cycle. An average of 2.3 cycles is developed per year with an approximate duration of 4.5 months per cycle. Around 2000 weaned piglets enter into the farm per cycle to produce 1960 pigs, which implies an approximate death rate of 2%. Weaned piglets are initially fed with a ratio of around 190 g of first finisher concentrate-day weaned piglet-¹. Then, second finisher concentrate is supplied to the pigs until the end of the cycle (100 kg live weight), with a ratio of 1.50 kg of second finisher concentrate.c day-1 pig The characteristics of concentrate feed used are also depicted in Table 1.
2.3.2.3. Common practices. Water troughs are used for water sup- ply, which come from an extraction well. Wastewater from both troughs and cleaning activities is sent to tanks where it is mixed

with manure. This mixture is directly valorised as organic fertiliser due to its nutrient content (0.48% N, 0.15% P). The emissions and discharges derived from manure management (including storage and application) as well as the environmental credits related to its application as an organic fertiliser (avoided mineral fertilisation) are included within the system boundaries. The extensions of agricultural soil used for the manure application from the weaning and fattening farms are 90 ha and 82.5 ha, respectively.

In the system assessed here, the main product (pig after fattening stage) has an important commercial value since it is suitable for subsequent pigment production. However, the manure excreted by the pigs can be also considered as a valuable co- product. Taking into account that avoiding allocation is recom- mended by ISO standard, manure was assumed to substitute syn- thetic mineral fertilisers, such that the credits related to the production and further application of avoided fertilisers can be assigned to its environmental profile. Thus, not only the environ- mental burdens associated with the storage and field application of manure were allocated to pigs rearing, but also the reduction in environmental loads due to the avoided mineral fertilisers pro- duction and application. According to Birkmose (2007), the mineral fertiliser equivalent (MFE) for N derived from an organic fertiliser is

75%. It means that every 100 kg of N applied as pig manure should replace 75 kg of N from mineral fertilisers (Nguyen et al., 2010). The MFE values for P and K in manure are considered 100% in most cases (Sommer et al., 2008). However, Dalgaard et al. (2006) stated that the substitution rate for P in manure should be adjusted to 97%, taking into account the potential P leaching from the cropping farms.

Moreover, it should be noted that allocation rules must be also defined in background processes responsible for the production of the different feed ingredients. Both mass and economic allocation was considered for the distribution of the environmental burdens in this case.

The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked to the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). The sources used to obtain the inventory data as well as the criteria assumed in the study are described below.

Concentrate feed supply for each pig category was analysed and inventoried in detail. Primary data about concentrate feed pro- duction and composition was provided by a Galician concentrate feed factory, which reflected information concerning 2014 (Table 1). According to the concentrate feed factory, most in- gredients (wheat, barley and other minor components) were pro- duced in Europe, mainly England, France and Spain, so average distances of 1200 km, 1500 km and 650 km between the feed company and the croplands, respectively, were assumed. The average transport distances between the concentrate feed company and the pig farms were estimated at 82 km for the weaning farm (Abegondo) and 100 km for the fattening farm (Lalín). Other

valuable ingredients were also imported. Maize was transported from Argentina, USA and Ukraine, whereas soymeal was shipped from Argentina, USA and Brazil. In this case, the distances between ports were calculated with a web distance calculator. 1 Lorries were considered for road transport, whereas bulk carriers were used for sea transport.

The inventory of concentrate feed production also comprises background data about crops cultivation, agrochemicals use and energy consumption. These additional data were mainly obtained from ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2014), except for soy cultivation, whose background data was taken and adapted from LCA food DK database (Nielsen et al., 2003).

Primary inventory data (production chain, concentrate feed ra- tios, water and energy consumption, etc.) for both farms were ob- tained through surveys and questionnaires fulfilled by growers as well as through visits to the farms. Measuring devices were used to record electricity requirements for the weaning farm in real time, whereas information about electricity consumption in the fattening farm was obtained from devices that measure free energy. In- ventory data per functional unit corresponding to the weaning and fattening farms are described in Table 2 and Table 3, respectively.

2.5.2.1. Emissions calculation. Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the farm activities were also calculated. Thus, the Tier 1 method proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) was applied to calculate CH4 emissions to air from both enteric fermentation and manure storage. For this,

(2006) Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment CH4|CH4|CH4|CH4 Enteric fermentation|150|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 1 Manure management|1.01|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 N20|100|g| NH3|2.23|kg| Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions NO|4.80|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 PO4|9.40|g|Rossier (1998) Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application N20|28.0|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 NH3|250|g| NO|2.35|kg| PO4|9.10|g|Rossier (1998)

primary data relative to the number of heads for each category of livestock (sows, piglets and fattening pigs) collected from both farms was used for calculations. Taking into account this informa- tion together with the default emission factors provided by the method, it was possible to determine the emissions of CH4 from the global system to the atmosphere.

Similarly, IPPC information (IPCC, 2006) was also considered to estimate the direct (N20) and indirect (NOx, NH3, NO3) nitrogen emissions derived from pig manure management. However, the Tier 2 method was followed as basis for calculation in this case. Information about heads and average mass of each category of livestock as well as the volume and density of manure was required. Moreover, a process in two stages was assumed regarding manure management: (i) pig manure storage within farm facilities and (ii) a further confinement in either tanks or earthen ponds where manure may be removed periodically. The default emissions factors (such as EF3 and EF4, among others) throughout calculations were selected in accordance with this information. Nitrogen emissions from manure application in soils as organic fertiliser were also determined by means of Tier 2 method, taking into account the same primary data. In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).
14: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Concentrate feed supply for each pig category was analysed and inventoried in detail. Primary data about concentrate feed pro- duction and composition was provided by a Galician concentrate feed factory, which reflected information concerning 2014 (Table 1). According to the concentrate feed factory, most in- gredients (wheat, barley and other minor components) were pro- duced in Europe, mainly England, France and Spain, so average distances of 1200 km, 1500 km and 650 km between the feed company and the croplands, respectively, were assumed. The average transport distances between the concentrate feed company and the pig farms were estimated at 82 km for the weaning farm (Abegondo) and 100 km for the fattening farm (Lalín). Other

valuable ingredients were also imported. Maize was transported from Argentina, USA and Ukraine, whereas soymeal was shipped from Argentina, USA and Brazil. In this case, the distances between ports were calculated with a web distance calculator. 1 Lorries were considered for road transport, whereas bulk carriers were used for sea transport.

The inventory of concentrate feed production also comprises background data about crops cultivation, agrochemicals use and energy consumption. These additional data were mainly obtained from ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2014), except for soy cultivation, whose background data was taken and adapted from LCA food DK database (Nielsen et al., 2003).

Primary inventory data (production chain, concentrate feed ra- tios, water and energy consumption, etc.) for both farms were ob- tained through surveys and questionnaires fulfilled by growers as well as through visits to the farms. Measuring devices were used to record electricity requirements for the weaning farm in real time, whereas information about electricity consumption in the fattening farm was obtained from devices that measure free energy. In- ventory data per functional unit corresponding to the weaning and fattening farms are described in Table 2 and Table 3, respectively.

2.5.2.1. Emissions calculation. Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the farm activities were also calculated. Thus, the Tier 1 method proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) was applied to calculate CH4 emissions to air from both enteric fermentation and manure storage. For this,

(2006) Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment CH4|CH4|CH4|CH4 Enteric fermentation|150|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 1 Manure management|1.01|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 N20|100|g| NH3|2.23|kg| Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions NO|4.80|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 PO4|9.40|g|Rossier (1998) Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application N20|28.0|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 NH3|250|g| NO|2.35|kg| PO4|9.10|g|Rossier (1998)

primary data relative to the number of heads for each category of livestock (sows, piglets and fattening pigs) collected from both farms was used for calculations. Taking into account this informa- tion together with the default emission factors provided by the method, it was possible to determine the emissions of CH4 from the global system to the atmosphere.

Similarly, IPPC information (IPCC, 2006) was also considered to estimate the direct (N20) and indirect (NOx, NH3, NO3) nitrogen emissions derived from pig manure management. However, the Tier 2 method was followed as basis for calculation in this case. Information about heads and average mass of each category of livestock as well as the volume and density of manure was required. Moreover, a process in two stages was assumed regarding manure management: (i) pig manure storage within farm facilities and (ii) a further confinement in either tanks or earthen ponds where manure may be removed periodically. The default emissions factors (such as EF3 and EF4, among others) throughout calculations were selected in accordance with this information. Nitrogen emissions from manure application in soils as organic fertiliser were also determined by means of Tier 2 method, taking into account the same primary data. In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).
16: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

After initial insemination, SOWS remain housed in the gestating section during approximately 110 days and then, they are moved to the farrowing section, 5 days before the provisional farrowing date. Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Due to the young age of piglets, optimal ambient conditions must be provided; thus, both electrical and thermal energy

Fig. 1. Scheme of the system boundaries for the pigs rearing system under assessment (Base Case - Scenario A). Black box represents the process excluded from the assessment. "Avoided mineral fertilisation" process includes both avoided environmental burdens due to mineral fertiliser production and emissions derived from its application to soil.
consumptions arise as key factors in the weaning stage (weaning farm).

Regarding feeding, piglets are fed for about 35 days with ratios of 105 g of first starter concentrate-day-1 piglet-¹ and 415 g of second starter concentrate-day piglet-1. The compositions of concentrate feed used in the weaning stage are displayed in Table 1.

2.3.2.2. Fattening farm. After the first stage, weaned piglets are transported (115 km far) to the fattening farm located in Lalin (08° 07' long. W, 42° 39 lat. N) with an extension around 2 ha. This second farm also operates under cycles, completing one fattening stage during each cycle. An average of 2.3 cycles is developed per year with an approximate duration of 4.5 months per cycle. Around 2000 weaned piglets enter into the farm per cycle to produce 1960 pigs, which implies an approximate death rate of 2%. Weaned piglets are initially fed with a ratio of around 190 g of first finisher concentrate-day weaned piglet-¹. Then, second finisher concentrate is supplied to the pigs until the end of the cycle (100 kg live weight), with a ratio of 1.50 kg of second finisher concentrate.c day-1 pig The characteristics of concentrate feed used are also depicted in Table 1.
2.3.2.3. Common practices. Water troughs are used for water sup- ply, which come from an extraction well. Wastewater from both troughs and cleaning activities is sent to tanks where it is mixed

with manure. This mixture is directly valorised as organic fertiliser due to its nutrient content (0.48% N, 0.15% P). The emissions and discharges derived from manure management (including storage and application) as well as the environmental credits related to its application as an organic fertiliser (avoided mineral fertilisation) are included within the system boundaries. The extensions of agricultural soil used for the manure application from the weaning and fattening farms are 90 ha and 82.5 ha, respectively.

In the system assessed here, the main product (pig after fattening stage) has an important commercial value since it is suitable for subsequent pigment production. However, the manure excreted by the pigs can be also considered as a valuable co- product. Taking into account that avoiding allocation is recom- mended by ISO standard, manure was assumed to substitute syn- thetic mineral fertilisers, such that the credits related to the production and further application of avoided fertilisers can be assigned to its environmental profile. Thus, not only the environ- mental burdens associated with the storage and field application of manure were allocated to pigs rearing, but also the reduction in environmental loads due to the avoided mineral fertilisers pro- duction and application. According to Birkmose (2007), the mineral fertiliser equivalent (MFE) for N derived from an organic fertiliser is

In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).
18: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

After initial insemination, SOWS remain housed in the gestating section during approximately 110 days and then, they are moved to the farrowing section, 5 days before the provisional farrowing date. Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Due to the young age of piglets, optimal ambient conditions must be provided; thus, both electrical and thermal energy

Fig. 1. Scheme of the system boundaries for the pigs rearing system under assessment (Base Case - Scenario A). Black box represents the process excluded from the assessment. "Avoided mineral fertilisation" process includes both avoided environmental burdens due to mineral fertiliser production and emissions derived from its application to soil.
consumptions arise as key factors in the weaning stage (weaning farm).

Regarding feeding, piglets are fed for about 35 days with ratios of 105 g of first starter concentrate-day-1 piglet-¹ and 415 g of second starter concentrate-day piglet-1. The compositions of concentrate feed used in the weaning stage are displayed in Table 1.

2.3.2.2. Fattening farm. After the first stage, weaned piglets are transported (115 km far) to the fattening farm located in Lalin (08° 07' long. W, 42° 39 lat. N) with an extension around 2 ha. This second farm also operates under cycles, completing one fattening stage during each cycle. An average of 2.3 cycles is developed per year with an approximate duration of 4.5 months per cycle. Around 2000 weaned piglets enter into the farm per cycle to produce 1960 pigs, which implies an approximate death rate of 2%. Weaned piglets are initially fed with a ratio of around 190 g of first finisher concentrate-day weaned piglet-¹. Then, second finisher concentrate is supplied to the pigs until the end of the cycle (100 kg live weight), with a ratio of 1.50 kg of second finisher concentrate.c day-1 pig The characteristics of concentrate feed used are also depicted in Table 1.
2.3.2.3. Common practices. Water troughs are used for water sup- ply, which come from an extraction well. Wastewater from both troughs and cleaning activities is sent to tanks where it is mixed

with manure. This mixture is directly valorised as organic fertiliser due to its nutrient content (0.48% N, 0.15% P). The emissions and discharges derived from manure management (including storage and application) as well as the environmental credits related to its application as an organic fertiliser (avoided mineral fertilisation) are included within the system boundaries. The extensions of agricultural soil used for the manure application from the weaning and fattening farms are 90 ha and 82.5 ha, respectively.

In the system assessed here, the main product (pig after fattening stage) has an important commercial value since it is suitable for subsequent pigment production. However, the manure excreted by the pigs can be also considered as a valuable co- product. Taking into account that avoiding allocation is recom- mended by ISO standard, manure was assumed to substitute syn- thetic mineral fertilisers, such that the credits related to the production and further application of avoided fertilisers can be assigned to its environmental profile. Thus, not only the environ- mental burdens associated with the storage and field application of manure were allocated to pigs rearing, but also the reduction in environmental loads due to the avoided mineral fertilisers pro- duction and application. According to Birkmose (2007), the mineral fertiliser equivalent (MFE) for N derived from an organic fertiliser is

75%. It means that every 100 kg of N applied as pig manure should replace 75 kg of N from mineral fertilisers (Nguyen et al., 2010). The MFE values for P and K in manure are considered 100% in most cases (Sommer et al., 2008). However, Dalgaard et al. (2006) stated that the substitution rate for P in manure should be adjusted to 97%, taking into account the potential P leaching from the cropping farms.

Moreover, it should be noted that allocation rules must be also defined in background processes responsible for the production of the different feed ingredients. Both mass and economic allocation was considered for the distribution of the environmental burdens in this case.

The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked to the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). The sources used to obtain the inventory data as well as the criteria assumed in the study are described below.

Concentrate feed supply for each pig category was analysed and inventoried in detail. Primary data about concentrate feed pro- duction and composition was provided by a Galician concentrate feed factory, which reflected information concerning 2014 (Table 1). According to the concentrate feed factory, most in- gredients (wheat, barley and other minor components) were pro- duced in Europe, mainly England, France and Spain, so average distances of 1200 km, 1500 km and 650 km between the feed company and the croplands, respectively, were assumed. The average transport distances between the concentrate feed company and the pig farms were estimated at 82 km for the weaning farm (Abegondo) and 100 km for the fattening farm (Lalín). Other

valuable ingredients were also imported. Maize was transported from Argentina, USA and Ukraine, whereas soymeal was shipped from Argentina, USA and Brazil. In this case, the distances between ports were calculated with a web distance calculator. 1 Lorries were considered for road transport, whereas bulk carriers were used for sea transport.

The inventory of concentrate feed production also comprises background data about crops cultivation, agrochemicals use and energy consumption. These additional data were mainly obtained from ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2014), except for soy cultivation, whose background data was taken and adapted from LCA food DK database (Nielsen et al., 2003).

Primary inventory data (production chain, concentrate feed ra- tios, water and energy consumption, etc.) for both farms were ob- tained through surveys and questionnaires fulfilled by growers as well as through visits to the farms. Measuring devices were used to record electricity requirements for the weaning farm in real time, whereas information about electricity consumption in the fattening farm was obtained from devices that measure free energy. In- ventory data per functional unit corresponding to the weaning and fattening farms are described in Table 2 and Table 3, respectively.

2.5.2.1. Emissions calculation. Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the farm activities were also calculated. Thus, the Tier 1 method proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) was applied to calculate CH4 emissions to air from both enteric fermentation and manure storage. For this,

primary data relative to the number of heads for each category of livestock (sows, piglets and fattening pigs) collected from both farms was used for calculations. Taking into account this informa- tion together with the default emission factors provided by the method, it was possible to determine the emissions of CH4 from the global system to the atmosphere.

Similarly, IPPC information (IPCC, 2006) was also considered to estimate the direct (N20) and indirect (NOx, NH3, NO3) nitrogen emissions derived from pig manure management. However, the Tier 2 method was followed as basis for calculation in this case. Information about heads and average mass of each category of livestock as well as the volume and density of manure was required. Moreover, a process in two stages was assumed regarding manure management: (i) pig manure storage within farm facilities and (ii) a further confinement in either tanks or earthen ponds where manure may be removed periodically. The default emissions factors (such as EF3 and EF4, among others) throughout calculations were selected in accordance with this information. Nitrogen emissions from manure application in soils as organic fertiliser were also determined by means of Tier 2 method, taking into account the same primary data. In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).

75%. It means that every 100 kg of N applied as pig manure should replace 75 kg of N from mineral fertilisers (Nguyen et al., 2010). The MFE values for P and K in manure are considered 100% in most cases (Sommer et al., 2008). However, Dalgaard et al. (2006) stated that the substitution rate for P in manure should be adjusted to 97%, taking into account the potential P leaching from the cropping farms.

Moreover, it should be noted that allocation rules must be also defined in background processes responsible for the production of the different feed ingredients. Both mass and economic allocation was considered for the distribution of the environmental burdens in this case.

The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked to the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). The sources used to obtain the inventory data as well as the criteria assumed in the study are described below.

Concentrate feed supply for each pig category was analysed and inventoried in detail. Primary data about concentrate feed pro- duction and composition was provided by a Galician concentrate feed factory, which reflected information concerning 2014 (Table 1). According to the concentrate feed factory, most in- gredients (wheat, barley and other minor components) were pro- duced in Europe, mainly England, France and Spain, so average distances of 1200 km, 1500 km and 650 km between the feed company and the croplands, respectively, were assumed. The average transport distances between the concentrate feed company and the pig farms were estimated at 82 km for the weaning farm (Abegondo) and 100 km for the fattening farm (Lalín). Other

valuable ingredients were also imported. Maize was transported from Argentina, USA and Ukraine, whereas soymeal was shipped from Argentina, USA and Brazil. In this case, the distances between ports were calculated with a web distance calculator. 1 Lorries were considered for road transport, whereas bulk carriers were used for sea transport.

The inventory of concentrate feed production also comprises background data about crops cultivation, agrochemicals use and energy consumption. These additional data were mainly obtained from ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2014), except for soy cultivation, whose background data was taken and adapted from LCA food DK database (Nielsen et al., 2003).

Primary inventory data (production chain, concentrate feed ra- tios, water and energy consumption, etc.) for both farms were ob- tained through surveys and questionnaires fulfilled by growers as well as through visits to the farms. Measuring devices were used to record electricity requirements for the weaning farm in real time, whereas information about electricity consumption in the fattening farm was obtained from devices that measure free energy. In- ventory data per functional unit corresponding to the weaning and fattening farms are described in Table 2 and Table 3, respectively.

2.5.2.1. Emissions calculation. Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the farm activities were also calculated. Thus, the Tier 1 method proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) was applied to calculate CH4 emissions to air from both enteric fermentation and manure storage. For this,

(2006) Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment CH4|CH4|CH4|CH4 Enteric fermentation|150|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 1 Manure management|1.01|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 N20|100|g| NH3|2.23|kg| Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions NO|4.80|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 PO4|9.40|g|Rossier (1998) Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application N20|28.0|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 NH3|250|g| NO|2.35|kg| PO4|9.10|g|Rossier (1998)

primary data relative to the number of heads for each category of livestock (sows, piglets and fattening pigs) collected from both farms was used for calculations. Taking into account this informa- tion together with the default emission factors provided by the method, it was possible to determine the emissions of CH4 from the global system to the atmosphere.

Similarly, IPPC information (IPCC, 2006) was also considered to estimate the direct (N20) and indirect (NOx, NH3, NO3) nitrogen emissions derived from pig manure management. However, the Tier 2 method was followed as basis for calculation in this case. Information about heads and average mass of each category of livestock as well as the volume and density of manure was required. Moreover, a process in two stages was assumed regarding manure management: (i) pig manure storage within farm facilities and (ii) a further confinement in either tanks or earthen ponds where manure may be removed periodically. The default emissions factors (such as EF3 and EF4, among others) throughout calculations were selected in accordance with this information. Nitrogen emissions from manure application in soils as organic fertiliser were also determined by means of Tier 2 method, taking into account the same primary data. In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).
21: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

After initial insemination, SOWS remain housed in the gestating section during approximately 110 days and then, they are moved to the farrowing section, 5 days before the provisional farrowing date. Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Due to the young age of piglets, optimal ambient conditions must be provided; thus, both electrical and thermal energy

Fig. 1. Scheme of the system boundaries for the pigs rearing system under assessment (Base Case - Scenario A). Black box represents the process excluded from the assessment. "Avoided mineral fertilisation" process includes both avoided environmental burdens due to mineral fertiliser production and emissions derived from its application to soil.
consumptions arise as key factors in the weaning stage (weaning farm).

Regarding feeding, piglets are fed for about 35 days with ratios of 105 g of first starter concentrate-day-1 piglet-¹ and 415 g of second starter concentrate-day piglet-1. The compositions of concentrate feed used in the weaning stage are displayed in Table 1.

2.3.2.2. Fattening farm. After the first stage, weaned piglets are transported (115 km far) to the fattening farm located in Lalin (08° 07' long. W, 42° 39 lat. N) with an extension around 2 ha. This second farm also operates under cycles, completing one fattening stage during each cycle. An average of 2.3 cycles is developed per year with an approximate duration of 4.5 months per cycle. Around 2000 weaned piglets enter into the farm per cycle to produce 1960 pigs, which implies an approximate death rate of 2%. Weaned piglets are initially fed with a ratio of around 190 g of first finisher concentrate-day weaned piglet-¹. Then, second finisher concentrate is supplied to the pigs until the end of the cycle (100 kg live weight), with a ratio of 1.50 kg of second finisher concentrate.c day-1 pig The characteristics of concentrate feed used are also depicted in Table 1.
2.3.2.3. Common practices. Water troughs are used for water sup- ply, which come from an extraction well. Wastewater from both troughs and cleaning activities is sent to tanks where it is mixed

with manure. This mixture is directly valorised as organic fertiliser due to its nutrient content (0.48% N, 0.15% P). The emissions and discharges derived from manure management (including storage and application) as well as the environmental credits related to its application as an organic fertiliser (avoided mineral fertilisation) are included within the system boundaries. The extensions of agricultural soil used for the manure application from the weaning and fattening farms are 90 ha and 82.5 ha, respectively.

In the system assessed here, the main product (pig after fattening stage) has an important commercial value since it is suitable for subsequent pigment production. However, the manure excreted by the pigs can be also considered as a valuable co- product. Taking into account that avoiding allocation is recom- mended by ISO standard, manure was assumed to substitute syn- thetic mineral fertilisers, such that the credits related to the production and further application of avoided fertilisers can be assigned to its environmental profile. Thus, not only the environ- mental burdens associated with the storage and field application of manure were allocated to pigs rearing, but also the reduction in environmental loads due to the avoided mineral fertilisers pro- duction and application. According to Birkmose (2007), the mineral fertiliser equivalent (MFE) for N derived from an organic fertiliser is

primary data relative to the number of heads for each category of livestock (sows, piglets and fattening pigs) collected from both farms was used for calculations. Taking into account this informa- tion together with the default emission factors provided by the method, it was possible to determine the emissions of CH4 from the global system to the atmosphere.

Similarly, IPPC information (IPCC, 2006) was also considered to estimate the direct (N20) and indirect (NOx, NH3, NO3) nitrogen emissions derived from pig manure management. However, the Tier 2 method was followed as basis for calculation in this case. Information about heads and average mass of each category of livestock as well as the volume and density of manure was required. Moreover, a process in two stages was assumed regarding manure management: (i) pig manure storage within farm facilities and (ii) a further confinement in either tanks or earthen ponds where manure may be removed periodically. The default emissions factors (such as EF3 and EF4, among others) throughout calculations were selected in accordance with this information. Nitrogen emissions from manure application in soils as organic fertiliser were also determined by means of Tier 2 method, taking into account the same primary data. In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).
23: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

After initial insemination, SOWS remain housed in the gestating section during approximately 110 days and then, they are moved to the farrowing section, 5 days before the provisional farrowing date. Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Due to the young age of piglets, optimal ambient conditions must be provided; thus, both electrical and thermal energy

Fig. 1. Scheme of the system boundaries for the pigs rearing system under assessment (Base Case - Scenario A). Black box represents the process excluded from the assessment. "Avoided mineral fertilisation" process includes both avoided environmental burdens due to mineral fertiliser production and emissions derived from its application to soil.
consumptions arise as key factors in the weaning stage (weaning farm).

Regarding feeding, piglets are fed for about 35 days with ratios of 105 g of first starter concentrate-day-1 piglet-¹ and 415 g of second starter concentrate-day piglet-1. The compositions of concentrate feed used in the weaning stage are displayed in Table 1.

2.3.2.2. Fattening farm. After the first stage, weaned piglets are transported (115 km far) to the fattening farm located in Lalin (08° 07' long. W, 42° 39 lat. N) with an extension around 2 ha. This second farm also operates under cycles, completing one fattening stage during each cycle. An average of 2.3 cycles is developed per year with an approximate duration of 4.5 months per cycle. Around 2000 weaned piglets enter into the farm per cycle to produce 1960 pigs, which implies an approximate death rate of 2%. Weaned piglets are initially fed with a ratio of around 190 g of first finisher concentrate-day weaned piglet-¹. Then, second finisher concentrate is supplied to the pigs until the end of the cycle (100 kg live weight), with a ratio of 1.50 kg of second finisher concentrate.c day-1 pig The characteristics of concentrate feed used are also depicted in Table 1.
2.3.2.3. Common practices. Water troughs are used for water sup- ply, which come from an extraction well. Wastewater from both troughs and cleaning activities is sent to tanks where it is mixed

with manure. This mixture is directly valorised as organic fertiliser due to its nutrient content (0.48% N, 0.15% P). The emissions and discharges derived from manure management (including storage and application) as well as the environmental credits related to its application as an organic fertiliser (avoided mineral fertilisation) are included within the system boundaries. The extensions of agricultural soil used for the manure application from the weaning and fattening farms are 90 ha and 82.5 ha, respectively.

In the system assessed here, the main product (pig after fattening stage) has an important commercial value since it is suitable for subsequent pigment production. However, the manure excreted by the pigs can be also considered as a valuable co- product. Taking into account that avoiding allocation is recom- mended by ISO standard, manure was assumed to substitute syn- thetic mineral fertilisers, such that the credits related to the production and further application of avoided fertilisers can be assigned to its environmental profile. Thus, not only the environ- mental burdens associated with the storage and field application of manure were allocated to pigs rearing, but also the reduction in environmental loads due to the avoided mineral fertilisers pro- duction and application. According to Birkmose (2007), the mineral fertiliser equivalent (MFE) for N derived from an organic fertiliser is

75%. It means that every 100 kg of N applied as pig manure should replace 75 kg of N from mineral fertilisers (Nguyen et al., 2010). The MFE values for P and K in manure are considered 100% in most cases (Sommer et al., 2008). However, Dalgaard et al. (2006) stated that the substitution rate for P in manure should be adjusted to 97%, taking into account the potential P leaching from the cropping farms.

Moreover, it should be noted that allocation rules must be also defined in background processes responsible for the production of the different feed ingredients. Both mass and economic allocation was considered for the distribution of the environmental burdens in this case.

The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked to the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). The sources used to obtain the inventory data as well as the criteria assumed in the study are described below.

Concentrate feed supply for each pig category was analysed and inventoried in detail. Primary data about concentrate feed pro- duction and composition was provided by a Galician concentrate feed factory, which reflected information concerning 2014 (Table 1). According to the concentrate feed factory, most in- gredients (wheat, barley and other minor components) were pro- duced in Europe, mainly England, France and Spain, so average distances of 1200 km, 1500 km and 650 km between the feed company and the croplands, respectively, were assumed. The average transport distances between the concentrate feed company and the pig farms were estimated at 82 km for the weaning farm (Abegondo) and 100 km for the fattening farm (Lalín). Other

valuable ingredients were also imported. Maize was transported from Argentina, USA and Ukraine, whereas soymeal was shipped from Argentina, USA and Brazil. In this case, the distances between ports were calculated with a web distance calculator. 1 Lorries were considered for road transport, whereas bulk carriers were used for sea transport.

The inventory of concentrate feed production also comprises background data about crops cultivation, agrochemicals use and energy consumption. These additional data were mainly obtained from ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2014), except for soy cultivation, whose background data was taken and adapted from LCA food DK database (Nielsen et al., 2003).

Primary inventory data (production chain, concentrate feed ra- tios, water and energy consumption, etc.) for both farms were ob- tained through surveys and questionnaires fulfilled by growers as well as through visits to the farms. Measuring devices were used to record electricity requirements for the weaning farm in real time, whereas information about electricity consumption in the fattening farm was obtained from devices that measure free energy. In- ventory data per functional unit corresponding to the weaning and fattening farms are described in Table 2 and Table 3, respectively.

Thus, the Tier 1 method proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) was applied to calculate CH4 emissions to air from both enteric fermentation and manure storage. Around 2000 weaned piglets enter into the farm per cycle to produce 1960 pigs, which implies an approximate death rate of 2%. Weaned piglets are initially fed with a ratio of around 190 g of first finisher concentrate-day weaned piglet-¹. Then, second finisher concentrate is supplied to the pigs until the end of the cycle (100 kg live weight), with a ratio of 1.50 kg of second finisher concentrate.c day-1 pig The characteristics of concentrate feed used are also depicted in Table 1.
2.3.2.3. Common practices. Water troughs are used for water sup- ply, which come from an extraction well. Wastewater from both troughs and cleaning activities is sent to tanks where it is mixed

with manure. This mixture is directly valorised as organic fertiliser due to its nutrient content (0.48% N, 0.15% P). The emissions and discharges derived from manure management (including storage and application) as well as the environmental credits related to its application as an organic fertiliser (avoided mineral fertilisation) are included within the system boundaries. The extensions of agricultural soil used for the manure application from the weaning and fattening farms are 90 ha and 82.5 ha, respectively.

In the system assessed here, the main product (pig after fattening stage) has an important commercial value since it is suitable for subsequent pigment production. However, the manure excreted by the pigs can be also considered as a valuable co- product. Taking into account that avoiding allocation is recom- mended by ISO standard, manure was assumed to substitute syn- thetic mineral fertilisers, such that the credits related to the production and further application of avoided fertilisers can be assigned to its environmental profile. Thus, not only the environ- mental burdens associated with the storage and field application of manure were allocated to pigs rearing, but also the reduction in environmental loads due to the avoided mineral fertilisers pro- duction and application. According to Birkmose (2007), the mineral fertiliser equivalent (MFE) for N derived from an organic fertiliser is

75%. It means that every 100 kg of N applied as pig manure should replace 75 kg of N from mineral fertilisers (Nguyen et al., 2010). (2006) stated that the substitution rate for P in manure should be adjusted to 97%, taking into account the potential P leaching from the cropping farms.

Moreover, it should be noted that allocation rules must be also defined in background processes responsible for the production of the different feed ingredients. Both mass and economic allocation was considered for the distribution of the environmental burdens in this case.

The relevance of an LCA is strongly linked to the quality of the inventory analysis (Basset-Mens et al., 2007). The sources used to obtain the inventory data as well as the criteria assumed in the study are described below.

Concentrate feed supply for each pig category was analysed and inventoried in detail. Primary data about concentrate feed pro- duction and composition was provided by a Galician concentrate feed factory, which reflected information concerning 2014 (Table 1). According to the concentrate feed factory, most in- gredients (wheat, barley and other minor components) were pro- duced in Europe, mainly England, France and Spain, so average distances of 1200 km, 1500 km and 650 km between the feed company and the croplands, respectively, were assumed. The average transport distances between the concentrate feed company and the pig farms were estimated at 82 km for the weaning farm (Abegondo) and 100 km for the fattening farm (Lalín). Other

valuable ingredients were also imported. Maize was transported from Argentina, USA and Ukraine, whereas soymeal was shipped from Argentina, USA and Brazil. In this case, the distances between ports were calculated with a web distance calculator. 1 Lorries were considered for road transport, whereas bulk carriers were used for sea transport.

The inventory of concentrate feed production also comprises background data about crops cultivation, agrochemicals use and energy consumption. These additional data were mainly obtained from ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2014), except for soy cultivation, whose background data was taken and adapted from LCA food DK database (Nielsen et al., 2003).

Primary inventory data (production chain, concentrate feed ra- tios, water and energy consumption, etc.) for both farms were ob- tained through surveys and questionnaires fulfilled by growers as well as through visits to the farms. Measuring devices were used to record electricity requirements for the weaning farm in real time, whereas information about electricity consumption in the fattening farm was obtained from devices that measure free energy. In- ventory data per functional unit corresponding to the weaning and fattening farms are described in Table 2 and Table 3, respectively.

2.5.2.1. Emissions calculation. Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the farm activities were also calculated. Thus, the Tier 1 method proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) was applied to calculate CH4 emissions to air from both enteric fermentation and manure storage. For this,

(2006) Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment CH4|CH4|CH4|CH4 Enteric fermentation|150|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 1 Manure management|1.01|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 N20|100|g| NH3|2.23|kg| Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions NO|4.80|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 PO4|9.40|g|Rossier (1998) Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application N20|28.0|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 NH3|250|g| NO|2.35|kg| PO4|9.10|g|Rossier (1998)

primary data relative to the number of heads for each category of livestock (sows, piglets and fattening pigs) collected from both farms was used for calculations. Taking into account this informa- tion together with the default emission factors provided by the method, it was possible to determine the emissions of CH4 from the global system to the atmosphere.

Similarly, IPPC information (IPCC, 2006) was also considered to estimate the direct (N20) and indirect (NOx, NH3, NO3) nitrogen emissions derived from pig manure management. However, the Tier 2 method was followed as basis for calculation in this case. Information about heads and average mass of each category of livestock as well as the volume and density of manure was required. Moreover, a process in two stages was assumed regarding manure management: (i) pig manure storage within farm facilities and (ii) a further confinement in either tanks or earthen ponds where manure may be removed periodically. The default emissions factors (such as EF3 and EF4, among others) throughout calculations were selected in accordance with this information. Nitrogen emissions from manure application in soils as organic fertiliser were also determined by means of Tier 2 method, taking into account the same primary data. In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).
26: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

After initial insemination, SOWS remain housed in the gestating section during approximately 110 days and then, they are moved to the farrowing section, 5 days before the provisional farrowing date. Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Due to the young age of piglets, optimal ambient conditions must be provided; thus, both electrical and thermal energy

consumptions arise as key factors in the weaning stage (weaning farm).

According to the concentrate feed factory, most in- gredients (wheat, barley and other minor components) were pro- duced in Europe, mainly England, France and Spain, so average distances of 1200 km, 1500 km and 650 km between the feed company and the croplands, respectively, were assumed. The average transport distances between the concentrate feed company and the pig farms were estimated at 82 km for the weaning farm (Abegondo) and 100 km for the fattening farm (Lalín). Other

valuable ingredients were also imported. Maize was transported from Argentina, USA and Ukraine, whereas soymeal was shipped from Argentina, USA and Brazil. In this case, the distances between ports were calculated with a web distance calculator. 1 Lorries were considered for road transport, whereas bulk carriers were used for sea transport.

The inventory of concentrate feed production also comprises background data about crops cultivation, agrochemicals use and energy consumption. These additional data were mainly obtained from ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2014), except for soy cultivation, whose background data was taken and adapted from LCA food DK database (Nielsen et al., 2003).

Primary inventory data (production chain, concentrate feed ra- tios, water and energy consumption, etc.) for both farms were ob- tained through surveys and questionnaires fulfilled by growers as well as through visits to the farms. Measuring devices were used to record electricity requirements for the weaning farm in real time, whereas information about electricity consumption in the fattening farm was obtained from devices that measure free energy. In- ventory data per functional unit corresponding to the weaning and fattening farms are described in Table 2 and Table 3, respectively.

2.5.2.1. Emissions calculation. Direct emissions to the atmosphere resulting from the farm activities were also calculated. Thus, the Tier 1 method proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006) was applied to calculate CH4 emissions to air from both enteric fermentation and manure storage. For this,

(2006) Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment CH4|CH4|CH4|CH4 Enteric fermentation|150|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 1 Manure management|1.01|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 N20|100|g| NH3|2.23|kg| Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions NO|4.80|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 PO4|9.40|g|Rossier (1998) Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application N20|28.0|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 NH3|250|g| NO|2.35|kg| PO4|9.10|g|Rossier (1998)

primary data relative to the number of heads for each category of livestock (sows, piglets and fattening pigs) collected from both farms was used for calculations. Taking into account this informa- tion together with the default emission factors provided by the method, it was possible to determine the emissions of CH4 from the global system to the atmosphere.

Similarly, IPPC information (IPCC, 2006) was also considered to estimate the direct (N20) and indirect (NOx, NH3, NO3) nitrogen emissions derived from pig manure management. However, the Tier 2 method was followed as basis for calculation in this case. Information about heads and average mass of each category of livestock as well as the volume and density of manure was required. Moreover, a process in two stages was assumed regarding manure management: (i) pig manure storage within farm facilities and (ii) a further confinement in either tanks or earthen ponds where manure may be removed periodically. The default emissions factors (such as EF3 and EF4, among others) throughout calculations were selected in accordance with this information. Nitrogen emissions from manure application in soils as organic fertiliser were also determined by means of Tier 2 method, taking into account the same primary data. In this line, inventory data derived from the production and use of avoided mineral fertilisers (urea, triple superphosphate and potassium sulphate) - due to the fertilising capacity of manure - were also considered, following again the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). Finally, PO4 emissions to water were calculated in accordance with the conversion factor of 0.01 kg of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998).
28: 2.3.2.1. Weaning farm. A farm located in Abegondo (8° 17' long. W, 43° 13 lat. N), Galicia (Northwest Spain), with an extension of around 21 ha, was selected to evaluate the piglets breeding stage. This weaning farm comprises a capacity of 4895 SOWS and 18 boars for the production of piglets up to 20 kg live weight. It operates in cycles to produce 10,595 piglets per breeding cycle, with an average of 9-10 cycles per year.

After initial insemination, SOWS remain housed in the gestating section during approximately 110 days and then, they are moved to the farrowing section, 5 days before the provisional farrowing date. Sows stay during the lactation period until the piglets are ready for weaning, that is, about 25 days of age and 6-7 kg live weight (Fig. After that, weaning pigs are reared until they reach the appropriate weight to leave the weaning farm for the feedlot (S2.2), approximately after 60 days and piglets weight of 20 kg live weight. Due to the young age of piglets, optimal ambient conditions must be provided; thus, both electrical and thermal energy

Fig. 1. Scheme of the system boundaries for the pigs rearing system under assessment (Base Case - Scenario A). Black box represents the process excluded from the assessment. "Avoided mineral fertilisation" process includes both avoided environmental burdens due to mineral fertiliser production and emissions derived from its application to soil.
consumptions arise as key factors in the weaning stage (weaning farm).

Regarding feeding, piglets are fed for about 35 days with ratios of 105 g of first starter concentrate-day-1 piglet-¹ and 415 g of second starter concentrate-day piglet-1. The compositions of concentrate feed used in the weaning stage are displayed in Table 1.

2.3.2.2. Fattening farm. After the first stage, weaned piglets are transported (115 km far) to the fattening farm located in Lalin (08° 07' long. W, 42° 39 lat. N) with an extension around 2 ha. This second farm also operates under cycles, completing one fattening stage during each cycle. An average of 2.3 cycles is developed per year with an approximate duration of 4.5 months per cycle. Around 2000 weaned piglets enter into the farm per cycle to produce 1960 pigs, which implies an approximate death rate of 2%. Weaned piglets are initially fed with a ratio of around 190 g of first finisher concentrate-day weaned piglet-¹. Then, second finisher concentrate is supplied to the pigs until the end of the cycle (100 kg live weight), with a ratio of 1.50 kg of second finisher concentrate.c day-1 pig The characteristics of concentrate feed used are also depicted in Table 1.
2.3.2.3. Common practices. Water troughs are used for water sup- ply, which come from an extraction well. Wastewater from both troughs and cleaning activities is sent to tanks where it is mixed

with manure. This mixture is directly valorised as organic fertiliser due to its nutrient content (0.48% N, 0.15% P). The emissions and discharges derived from manure management (including storage and application) as well as the environmental credits related to its application as an organic fertiliser (avoided mineral fertilisation) are included within the system boundaries. The extensions of agricultural soil used for the manure application from the weaning and fattening farms are 90 ha and 82.5 ha, respectively.

In the system assessed here, the main product (pig after fattening stage) has an important commercial value since it is suitable for subsequent pigment production. However, the manure excreted by the pigs can be also considered as a valuable co- product. Taking into account that avoiding allocation is recom- mended by ISO standard, manure was assumed to substitute syn- thetic mineral fertilisers, such that the credits related to the production and further application of avoided fertilisers can be assigned to its environmental profile. Thus, not only the environ- mental burdens associated with the storage and field application of manure were allocated to pigs rearing, but also the reduction in environmental loads due to the avoided mineral fertilisers pro- duction and application. According to Birkmose (2007), the mineral fertiliser equivalent (MFE) for N derived from an organic fertiliser is

30: Among the stages defined within the life cycle impact assess- ment phase of the standardised LCA methodology, classification and characterisation stages were undertaken in this study (ISO 14044, 2006). Classification is the assignment of the inventory re- sults to the different impact categories selected for evaluation; characterisation consists on calculating the final results of the in- dicators of these categories taking into account the characterisation factors (ISO 14044, 2006). In this sense, the following impact cat- legories were assessed: climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME) and agricultural land occupation (ALO). Results were also expressed in terms of fossil depletion (FD) in order to identify the use of non- renewable resources throughout the life cycle system. These impact categories were selected in agreement with previous studies on livestock production systems (Reckmann et al., 2012). Characteri- sation factors reported by the ReCipE Midpoint (H) 1.12 method were considered for all impact categories (Goedkoop et al., 2013a). The software SimaPro v8.2 was used for the computational implementation of the inventories (Goedkoop et al., 2013b).

Additionally, the life cycle inventory data was also used to assess the edible protein energy return on investment ratio (ep-EROI). The rationale behind this is mainly that the combination of this ratio with the LCA methodology allows a reduction of uncertainties in calculations (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2014). The ep-EROI sheds light on the efficiency of food systems taking into account the energy that food provides human consumers and the energy requirements
Table 3 Inventory data per functional unit (100 kg live weight of pig at farm gate) corresponding to the fattening farm (S2.2).

(2006) Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment|Air Outputs emissions to environment CH4|CH4|CH4|CH4 Enteric fermentation|555|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 1 Manure management|3.70|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 N20|250|g| NH3|5.43|kg| Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions|Water emissions NO|11.7|kg|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 PO4|23.0|g|Rossier (1998) Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application|Avoided fertiliser application N20|68.0|g|IPCC (2006) Tier 2 NH3|530|g| NO|5.75|kg| PO4|22.0|g|Rossier (1998)

for food production (Pimentel and Pimentel, 2003; Tyedmers, 2004; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2014). Thus, it was estimated by dividing the protein energy output of the pigment obtained in the system and the energy inputs related to the rearing up to farm gate (pigs to slaughterhouse). The embodied energy in pigment (energy output) was calculated based on the protein content per edible portion per functional unit (100 kg live weight of pig at farm gate). Considering the following data for calculations: 57% yield of carcass pigment per kg live weight, protein content of 11.2% per kg of carcass pigmeat and an energy density of 16.73 MJ per kg of protein (FAO, 2007; ODAFF, 2014), an energy output of 1.07 MJ per kg live weight was estimated. Regarding energy inputs, the Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) indicator proposed by VDI-Richtlinien

(1997) was selected to determine the energy requirements of the global system. The CED represents the primary energy used throughout the entire life cycle of a product, including the energy consumed from extraction, manufacturing and disposal activities (VDI-Richtlinien, 1997).

Impact categories|Units|Weaning farm|Fattening farm|Total Climate change (CC)|kg CO2 eq|40.0|302|342 Terrestrial acidification (TA)|kg SO2 eq|5.09|13.5|18.6 Freshwater eutrophication (FE)|kg eq|1.5.10|1.8.10|1.95.10-2 Marine eutrophication (ME)|kg N eq|0.98|4.03|5.01 Agricultural land occupation (ALO)|m² yr|39.5|456|496 Fossil depletion (FD)|kg oil eq|2.54|27.3|29.8

account the non-renewable energy consumption. Table 4 provides a summary including not only ep-EROI result obtained in the cur- rent study but also available ep-EROI values reported in literature (Pimentel and Pimentel, 2003; Iribarren et al., 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2013). Although two ep-EROI values are presented, depending on the energy requirements (renewable and non-renewable sources) assumed as energy input of the system, only ep-EROI results based on non-renewable energy requirements could be compared with literature data, due to the absence of studies based on total energy consumption as energy input. Ac- cording to the results, it should be noted that the own ep-EROI value (7.31%) is in line with those obtained for pigmeat produc- tion in other countries (United States). However, it is important to highlight the liability when comparing diverse studies conducted in different countries, because the discrepancies about calculation assumptions (boneless meat and carcass meat basis for calculation) limit the comparability of the ep-EROI results. Consequently, data adjustments in terms of energy requirements and pigmeat energy contents must be made to perform a reliable assessment.

Regarding other protein sources within Spain boundaries, literature suggests that livestock products, that is, pigmeat and milk, show similar ep-EROI results, with values ranging from 7.10% to 8.96% (Table 4). Similar scores can be also found for aquaculture products such as mussels (6.90%), while slightly higher results are registered relative to other fishing species like Patagonian grena- dier (10.4%) based on trawling techniques (Table 4).

Although two ep-EROI values are presented, depending on the energy requirements (renewable and non-renewable sources) assumed as energy input of the system, only ep-EROI results based on non-renewable energy requirements could be compared with literature data, due to the absence of studies based on total energy consumption as energy input.
