However, it is important to highlight the liability when comparing diverse studies conducted in different countries, because the discrepancies about calculation assumptions (boneless meat and carcass meat basis for calculation) limit the comparability of the ep-EROI results. Consequently, data adjustments in terms of energy requirements and pigmeat energy contents must be made to perform a reliable assessment.

39: Table 5 shows the characterisation results for all impact cate- gories for the entire system. Environmental impacts related to the weaning and fattening farms are also presented individually. Ac- cording to the results, fattening farm activities contributed to most environmental impact categories (with contributions exceeding 72%), except for ALO, where impacts to soil associated with the weaning farm were more significant. Therefore, the environmental burdens associated with the fattening phase are decisive. These results agree with other LCA studies including pig rearing and it can be explained by the fact that the period from weaning to slaugh- tering (fattening time) is longer than the piglets weaning period (Reckmann et al., 2012). Another explanation is related to the weight of the pigs throughout the different stages. The herd on fattening farm (post-weaning piglets and fattening pigs) has a
higher weight, so that both the requirements of concentrate feed and the amount of manure increase (Reckmann et al., 2012).

Since severe differences were found in the environmental pro- file of both stages (weaning farm and fattening farm), an in-depth assessment of each farm was performed separately. Thus, it was possible to evaluate the burdens associated with each stage as well as to identify their most critical activities (hotspots). Details about the assessment performed are reported below.

41: <header>3.2.1. The environmental analysis of the weaning farm </header>

Table 6 displays the contributions to the six impact categories from each contributing process involved in the weaning farm stage. According to the results, two critical environmental factors can be distinguished: concentrate feed production and on-farm emissions associated with farming activities. The contribution from on-farm emissions mainly comprises the emissions associated to the farm (such as CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation during herd confinement) as well as the manure management process.

Environmental impacts related to concentrate feed production (S1.1 and S1.2) were relevant in almost all impact categories (except for TA), ranging from 14% to 78% depending on the category. On- farm emissions factor resulted also in important contributions not only in categories such as CC, TA, FE and ME but also in ALO (with contributions around 24%). This fact due to on-farm emis- sions factor also includes the impacts of land use associated with farm infrastructure and agricultural soil destined to manure application.

Conversely, it was stated that the application of manure excreted by piglets avoids the production of a certain amount of N and P and, therefore, the production of mineral fertilisers based on these compounds. In this sense, consideration of both avoided mineral fertilisers production (i.e. avoided production) and avoided mineral fertilisers application (i.e. avoided emissions) represent environmental credits in most impact categories, contributing to reductions up to 32% of the environmental impacts.

47: During the last few years, the number of LCA studies for live- stock production has increased. However, these studies vary considerably in their level of detail, their definition of system boundaries, the emission factors used and other technical aspects such as allocation approach or functional unit considered (Reckmann et al., 2012). Therefore, only those LCA studies that (i) were conducted in OECD countries based on intensive growing systems, (iii) involving all production stages up to slaughterhouse (at least) and (iv) sharing mass FU and similar allocation rules (systems expansion) were identified to be suitable for comparison with the present work.

According to these criteria, six LCA studies were considered. Since remaining discrepancies in terms of scope definition were detected among the studies selected, some characterisation values had to be recalculated. Thus, the final LCA results of the different studies with identical system boundaries (cradle-to-gate farm) and functional unit (1 kg live weight of pig) are presented in Table 8.

According to the data shown in Table 8, similarities among the different studies were obtained in terms of CC, with values ranging from 2.25 (Dourmad et al., 2014) to 3.42 kg CO2 eq per kg live weight (present study). Since N20 emissions was found as the main contributor to CC, the criteria used by the different authors to es- timate N20 emissions is considered as one of the main factors responsible for the slight differences in CC values. Likewise, FD results did not differ much (less than 31%) between the present study (12.5 MJ per kg live weight) and the values reported in the literature (ranging from 15.1 to 18.1 MJ).
When focusing on ALO, it was found that discrepancies were intimately linked to the selection of system boundaries in terms of land occupation. According to Nguyen et al. (2010), farming units

can be considered as landless and only the land use related to the cultivation of concentrate feed ingredients was taken into account. In contrast, the area of land occupied by farm facilities and those used to the application of manure excreted by pigs were also considered in this study, so that higher ALO results were registered (4.96 m² yr) in comparison with the values obtained when only land use associated with concentrate feed production was accounted (3.03 m² yr). In both cases, ALO results were in line with those reported in literature (ranging from 4.40 m² yr to 6.55 m² yr).

TA values obtained in the present study (186 g SO2 eq per kg live weight) lie above the range of values reported in literature (35.1-56.5 SO2 eq per kg live weight). Earlier studies about pig- meat production showed that acidification was mainly caused by NH3 emissions, so that differences in TA can be explained by dif- ferences in NH3 ratios. In this sense, different NH3 emission factors were used in LCA studies for different types of housing, manure storage and manure application techniques, so that this question is decisive for the variability in acidification results. Moreover, it was found that NH3 emissions are intimately related to the production of concentrate feed ingredients. Thus, the activities developed in cereals cultivation (field preparation, agrochemicals application together with concentrate feed composition and consumption on farms also accounted for the difference in TA values.

It should be noted that the use of different characterisation methodologies could lead to disparities in the final characterisation results. Although this issue can affect all impact categories under assessment, its influence will be crucial in terms of eutrophication potential. Thus, mismatched results expressed in different units were recorded for EP in the literature, due to the differences on the characterisation factors as well as on the substances included per impact category. ReCiPe methodology was selected for data assessment. According to this method, eutrophication potential was expressed by two impact categories, freshwater eutrophication (FE) and marine eutrophication (ME), in terms of equivalent emissions of P and N, respectively. Different characterisation methodologies were selected by other authors, where only one impact category is used to estimate the eutrophication potential (from now, EP). Thus, a CML methodology was used by Reckmann et al. (2013) and Dourmad et al. (2014) for data assessment, expressing the characterisation results as g PO4 eq, whereas Dalgaard et al. (2007) and Nguyen et al. (2011) chose the EDIP, a specific Danish methodology, using units of g NO3 eq for the eutrophication results.

In order to achieve a more reliable comparison, the evaluation of the inventory data based on CML and EDIP methodologies was also carried out. Table 9 shows the eutrophication results based on the different methodologies considered. According to the data, the EP value obtained with CML methodology (45.7 g PO43 eq per kg live weight) lies partly outside the values available in literature (19.0-21.4 PO4 eq per kg live weight). However, when EDIP methodology was taken into consideration (see Table 9), EP results were in line with reported data elsewhere (Dalgaard et al., 2007).
According to the above, it could be concluded that our results
48: During the last few years, the number of LCA studies for live- stock production has increased. However, these studies vary considerably in their level of detail, their definition of system boundaries, the emission factors used and other technical aspects such as allocation approach or functional unit considered (Reckmann et al., 2012). Therefore, only those LCA studies that (i) were conducted in OECD countries based on intensive growing systems, (iii) involving all production stages up to slaughterhouse (at least) and (iv) sharing mass FU and similar allocation rules (systems expansion) were identified to be suitable for comparison with the present work.

According to these criteria, six LCA studies were considered. Since remaining discrepancies in terms of scope definition were detected among the studies selected, some characterisation values had to be recalculated. Thus, the final LCA results of the different studies with identical system boundaries (cradle-to-gate farm) and functional unit (1 kg live weight of pig) are presented in Table 8.

According to the data shown in Table 8, similarities among the different studies were obtained in terms of CC, with values ranging from 2.25 (Dourmad et al., 2014) to 3.42 kg CO2 eq per kg live weight (present study). Since N20 emissions was found as the main contributor to CC, the criteria used by the different authors to es- timate N20 emissions is considered as one of the main factors responsible for the slight differences in CC values. Likewise, FD results did not differ much (less than 31%) between the present study (12.5 MJ per kg live weight) and the values reported in the literature (ranging from 15.1 to 18.1 MJ).
When focusing on ALO, it was found that discrepancies were intimately linked to the selection of system boundaries in terms of land occupation. According to Nguyen et al. (2010), farming units

can be considered as landless and only the land use related to the cultivation of concentrate feed ingredients was taken into account. In contrast, the area of land occupied by farm facilities and those used to the application of manure excreted by pigs were also considered in this study, so that higher ALO results were registered (4.96 m² yr) in comparison with the values obtained when only land use associated with concentrate feed production was accounted (3.03 m² yr). In both cases, ALO results were in line with those reported in literature (ranging from 4.40 m² yr to 6.55 m² yr).

TA values obtained in the present study (186 g SO2 eq per kg live weight) lie above the range of values reported in literature (35.1-56.5 SO2 eq per kg live weight). Earlier studies about pig- meat production showed that acidification was mainly caused by NH3 emissions, so that differences in TA can be explained by dif- ferences in NH3 ratios. In this sense, different NH3 emission factors were used in LCA studies for different types of housing, manure storage and manure application techniques, so that this question is decisive for the variability in acidification results. Moreover, it was found that NH3 emissions are intimately related to the production of concentrate feed ingredients. Thus, the activities developed in cereals cultivation (field preparation, agrochemicals application together with concentrate feed composition and consumption on farms also accounted for the difference in TA values.

a Considering a conversion factor of 0.78 kg carcass weight-kg live weight. b CC: climate change; TA: terrestrial acidification; FE: freshwater eutrophication; ME: marine eutrophication; EP: eutrophication; ALO: agricultural land occupation; FD: fossil depletion. are consistent with current European LCA studies in terms of CC, FD and ALO, provided that identical system boundaries are considered. Slight differences were obtained for EP values when EDIP meth- odology was considered for calculations. In contrast, higher dif- ferences were registered in TA as well as in EP results when CML methodology was selected, although these discrepancies could be justified by the influence of agricultural practices during cultivation of concentrate ingredients.
According to the results in the base scenario, concentrate feed production was registered as the most critical environmental factor (hotspot) in the environmental profile of both farms. More

specifically, the production of the concentrate feed ingredients (maize, wheat, soy meal and barley) was the process with a critical role in all the impact categories under assessment (Figs. A1 - A4, Appendix). These contributions are mainly associated with the emissions derived from the production and use of mineral fertil- isers and later application on cultivation soils. The transport also considerably influences most categories (CC, TA, FD) with contri- butions ranging from 12% to 22%. It is mainly due to the large transport distances for feed ingredients.

In this context, a specific assessment of the burdens associated with this stage was carried out. Thus, two alternative scenarios based on changes on mixtures composition were evaluated with the aim of analysing potential environmental improvements in terms of concentrate feed production.

a Considering a conversion factor of 0.78 kg carcass weight-kg live weight. b CC: climate change; TA: terrestrial acidification; FE: freshwater eutrophication; ME: marine eutrophication; EP: eutrophication; ALO: agricultural land occupation; FD: fossil depletion. are consistent with current European LCA studies in terms of CC, FD and ALO, provided that identical system boundaries are considered. Slight differences were obtained for EP values when EDIP meth- odology was considered for calculations. In contrast, higher dif- ferences were registered in TA as well as in EP results when CML methodology was selected, although these discrepancies could be justified by the influence of agricultural practices during cultivation of concentrate ingredients.
According to the results in the base scenario, concentrate feed production was registered as the most critical environmental factor (hotspot) in the environmental profile of both farms. More

specifically, the production of the concentrate feed ingredients (maize, wheat, soy meal and barley) was the process with a critical role in all the impact categories under assessment (Figs. A1 - A4, Appendix). These contributions are mainly associated with the emissions derived from the production and use of mineral fertil- isers and later application on cultivation soils. The transport also considerably influences most categories (CC, TA, FD) with contri- butions ranging from 12% to 22%. It is mainly due to the large transport distances for feed ingredients.

In this context, a specific assessment of the burdens associated with this stage was carried out. Thus, two alternative scenarios based on changes on mixtures composition were evaluated with the aim of analysing potential environmental improvements in terms of concentrate feed production.

In this context, a specific assessment of the burdens associated with this stage was carried out. Thus, two alternative scenarios based on changes on mixtures composition were evaluated with the aim of analysing potential environmental improvements in terms of concentrate feed production.

According to the results in the base scenario, concentrate feed production was registered as the most critical environmental factor (hotspot) in the environmental profile of both farms. More

specifically, the production of the concentrate feed ingredients (maize, wheat, soy meal and barley) was the process with a critical role in all the impact categories under assessment (Figs. A1 - A4, Appendix). These contributions are mainly associated with the emissions derived from the production and use of mineral fertil- isers and later application on cultivation soils. 3.4.1. Environmental burdens of concentrate feed mixtures taking into account Italian maize and wheat as concentrate feed ingredients

In this study, regarding calculations of base scenario (Scenario A), inventory concerning background data about ingredients cultivation were taken from ecoinvent (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Käggi, 2007) -where average agricultural practices for Europe are considered- as well as LCA food DK databases (Nielsen et al., 2003). Moreover, according to concentrated feed formulations in the base scenario, maize is imported from Ukraine, USA and Argentina whereas wheat is transported from United Kingdom, France and Spain.

Since Northern Italy is known as a suitable region to produce agricultural crops with high potential yields (Noya et al., 2015), an alternative scenario based on Italian maize and wheat as in- gredients in concentrate feed formulations was proposed (Scenario B).

Comparative profiles for the global system for both scenarios (Scenario A and Scenario B) are shown in Fig. 2. According to the results, the consideration of the Italian maize and wheat would cause a decrease in the environmental burdens, independently of
Table 10
Main ingredients and composition of the alternative second finisher concentrate formulation (Scenario B) based on grain legumes as main protein source (Baumgartner et al., 2008).

Ingredients|Alternative second finisher concentrate Wheat (%)|30.8 Maize (%)|20.9 Barley (%)|13.2 Peas (%)|22.0 Animal fats (%)|2.2 Rapeseed (%)|6.2 DDGS (%)|2.6 Organic compounds|1.3 Inorganic compounds|0.8
a DDGS - Distiller's dried grains with solubles.

the impact category. However, it would be possible to observe some specific categories in which the reduced impact could result really significant: FE and ME. This is mainly attributed to the lower re- quirements of agrochemicals during maize and wheat cultivation under Italian conditions in comparison with those used in the base scenario (average for European countries). Thus, whereas higher doses of N and P based fertilisers are required in the cultivation of maize in scenario A (8.63 g N/kg maize and 0.68 g P/kg maize, respectively), low amounts of these fertilisers are applied in Italian crop lands (with rates around 1.85 g N/kg maize and no application of P based fertilisers). Similarly for wheat, around 19.6 g N/kg wheat and 2.05 g P/kg wheat of N and P based fertilisers are applied in European wheat (average) compared to the values of 8.86 g N/kg wheat and absence of P fertilisers in Italian conditions.

Lower influence (up to 19%) would be registered in the other impact categories (CC, TA, ALO and FD) compared to scenario A. It would be mainly due to the similarities in terms of agricultural activities during cultivation practices in both scenarios. Certainly, lower fossil fuels consumptions related to transport of maize and wheat to the concentrate factory would be responsible of reducing the environmental burdens of the global system.

65: <header>3.4.2. Alternative second finisher fodder composition in terms of protein sources </header>

The second finisher concentrate supplied to fattening pigs was the concentrate feed mixture of major importance in terms of global characterisation results per functional unit. It was identified as the main responsible for the environmental burdens associated to the fattening farm which, in turn, was defined as the highest contributor to the global environmental impacts. Therefore, special attention should be paid to this area, in order to establish im- provements that can reduce emission levels.

In Europe there is a need for high-quality protein for animal concentrate feed and human consumption (Baumgartner et al., 2008). However, soy meal, the main protein source in current concentrate feed formulations, is also one of the factors with remarkable environmental impact. In this context, Baumgartner

Fig. 3. Comparative global environmental results considering two different formulations for the second finisher concentrate production. Scenario A: standard concentrate with soy meal as main protein source. Scenario C: alternative concentrate with peas as main protein source. Results are expressed as relative percentages. Impact category acronyms: CC = climate change; TA = terrestrial acidification; FE = freshwater eutrophication; ME = marine eutrophication; ALO = agricultural land occupation; FD = fossil depletion.

et al. (2008) propose the use of grain legumes (such as peas, beans and lupines) as an alternative protein source in concentrate feed formulations. According to these authors, this alternative ingre- dient could decrease the environmental burdens related to soy meal production, mainly because the cultivation of grain legumes does not require mineral fertilisation activities. Therefore, two formulations were compared in this study for the second finisher concentrate: (i) the standard concentrate (Scenario A), where soy meal is used as main protein source, and (ii) the alternative concentrate (Scenario C), where soy meal is replaced by peas in the second finisher concentrate formulation. A crude protein level of about 46% and 21% was considered for soy meal and peas, respec- tively (Baumgartner et al., 2008). Consequently, the ratios of the other ingredients were reformulated in order to preserve an adequate nutritional balance. Composition data concerning alter- native concentrate was reported in Table 10.

Comparative profiles for the fattening farm in both scenarios (Scenario A and Scenario C) are shown in Fig. 3. According to the results, it could be concluded that the use of an alternative concentrate based on peas as the main protein source (Scenario C) offers minor potential environmental improvements in CC, TA and FD in comparison with the standard concentrate. It is mainly due to the altered composition of energy-rich ingredients by which lower energy requirements are recorded as well as lower CO2 emissions from land transformation during cultivation processes. In contrast, higher environmental burdens are registered for FE, ME and ALO. The negative effects on FE (18% increase) and ME (3% increase) are related to the greater phosphate and nitrate losses during the cultivation of peas, whereas the low yield of peas combined with its higher proportion in alternative concentrate formulation led to more land occupation (about 29%) compared to the standard sce- nario (Baumgartner et al., 2008).

Fig. 3. Comparative global environmental results considering two different formulations for the second finisher concentrate production. Scenario A: standard concentrate with soy meal as main protein source. Scenario C: alternative concentrate with peas as main protein source. Results are expressed as relative percentages. Impact category acronyms: CC = climate change; TA = terrestrial acidification; FE = freshwater eutrophication; ME = marine eutrophication; ALO = agricultural land occupation; FD = fossil depletion.

et al. (2008) propose the use of grain legumes (such as peas, beans and lupines) as an alternative protein source in concentrate feed formulations. According to these authors, this alternative ingre- dient could decrease the environmental burdens related to soy meal production, mainly because the cultivation of grain legumes does not require mineral fertilisation activities. Therefore, two formulations were compared in this study for the second finisher concentrate: (i) the standard concentrate (Scenario A), where soy meal is used as main protein source, and (ii) the alternative concentrate (Scenario C), where soy meal is replaced by peas in the second finisher concentrate formulation. A crude protein level of about 46% and 21% was considered for soy meal and peas, respec- tively (Baumgartner et al., 2008). Consequently, the ratios of the other ingredients were reformulated in order to preserve an adequate nutritional balance. Composition data concerning alter- native concentrate was reported in Table 10.

Comparative profiles for the fattening farm in both scenarios (Scenario A and Scenario C) are shown in Fig. 3. According to the results, it could be concluded that the use of an alternative concentrate based on peas as the main protein source (Scenario C) offers minor potential environmental improvements in CC, TA and FD in comparison with the standard concentrate. It is mainly due to the altered composition of energy-rich ingredients by which lower energy requirements are recorded as well as lower CO2 emissions from land transformation during cultivation processes. In contrast, higher environmental burdens are registered for FE, ME and ALO. The negative effects on FE (18% increase) and ME (3% increase) are related to the greater phosphate and nitrate losses during the cultivation of peas, whereas the low yield of peas combined with its higher proportion in alternative concentrate formulation led to more land occupation (about 29%) compared to the standard sce- nario (Baumgartner et al., 2008).
67: <header>3.4.2. Alternative second finisher fodder composition in terms of protein sources </header>

The second finisher concentrate supplied to fattening pigs was the concentrate feed mixture of major importance in terms of global characterisation results per functional unit. It was identified as the main responsible for the environmental burdens associated to the fattening farm which, in turn, was defined as the highest contributor to the global environmental impacts. Therefore, special attention should be paid to this area, in order to establish im- provements that can reduce emission levels.

69: Pigmeat production emerges as the second contributor of GHG emission from livestock sector, which highlights the necessity to

assess the environmental impacts related to pigmeat supply chain. Several LCA studies involving European livestock produc- tion systems have been published to date, but fewer have focused on pigmeat production. In this study, a cradle-to-farm-gate anal- ysis was carried out to evaluate the environmental profile of weaning and fattening pig farms in Galicia (Northwest Spain). According to the results, the fattening farm related activities contributed most to the environmental burdens in almost all the impact categories (with contributions above 72%). Moreover, the concentrate feed production was found as the main environ- mental hotspot, followed by the emissions derived from both on- farm activities and manure management practices. These results were in accordance with similar European LCA studies involving pigmeat production.

Accordingly, mitigation strategies focused on concentrate feed production were proposed and two main conclusions were ob- tained: (i) the promotion of ingredients (maize and wheat) culti- vated in close regions reduces the environmental burdens while (ii) no evidence was found of potential environmental credits from the use of alternative protein sources in concentrate feed mixtures.
